# Extracao: transcription_mp4_04-Using_Equity_Financing_for_Your_Acquisition

---

In this lesson, we're talking about equity financing. Equity financing involves selling a portion of the company's equity in return for capital. You're selling your equity to a third party in exchange for cash needed to purchase the business. Let's start by covering the advantages of equity financing. The main advantage of equity financing is that there's no obligation to repay the money. You're taking a partner into the business that owns the business with you in exchange for the cash needed to buy the business. In the short term, that's great because it takes pressure off the business and allows you to use all the other debt financing vehicles that we talked about. But in the long term, it can be very frustrating if you give a lot of value away to somebody that gave you early stage money and then you built a huge business because you're going to have to buy them out at some point or they're going to want you to sell the business at some point so that they can recover the money they invested plus the upside that they wanted to have. When you think about equity financing, you're truly creating a partnership with a third party who's going to benefit from your work. Another upside to equity financing is that depending on the type of equity financing you use, there are far less restrictive covenants and hoops to jump through. In most cases, you'll be working directly with an investor versus working with a bank. Under the right circumstances, equity financing can be a win-win scenario for the seller, the buyer, and the investor. With equity financing, it is easier to buy a business because you're demonstrating to a seller that you have the credibility that someone's willing to invest with you. Therefore they know the cash is available to close the deal. It's a win-win for the third party investor too if you do your job. They provide the cash in exchange for equity in the business and the future profits of the business or gain in the equity value in the business. Another advantage to equity financing is if it's with the right equity investor, they may have experience in your industry. They could be a mentor. They actually may understand how to run a business so they might be more actively involved with helping you shape that business to success. There's a lot of advantages to having an equity partner if they're the right partner. One of the biggest advantages for you as the buyers, you're not putting very much money of your own down. Therefore it's less risky for you. You won't have the pressure of the loans and the potential loan fails to happen to people who borrow money from the bank. You'll feel less heavy when you take the business over and you'll have less risk. But you've inherited a partner. Now you have to learn how to manage and deal with a partner who's going to be actively interested in your business as well. If you go this route, I want to make sure you are fully aware of the disadvantages of this financing strategy. As I already said, the biggest downside is that your ownership share would decrease because you're giving away some stake in the company in return for money to buy the business. You'll have a partner in the business. They'll be an actively interested partner. And as someone who's had dozens of business partners, you'll want to make sure you choose that partner wisely. A business partnership in many ways is like a marriage. I recommend that you hire a really good lawyer who has experience in writing partnership agreements. You want to make sure this document is thorough and clearly outlines both you and your partner's expectations and responsibilities. You also want to ensure you think through what happens if either of you choose to dissolve the partnership or exit the business. It will save you an enormous amount of time, money, and energy in the future if you know what your exit looks like in advance. The worst thing you can do is get tied up in a dispute with a partner where you're litigating because that will damage your business. Many of these agreements also outline who makes the day-to-day decisions in the business, how you invest or distribute money from the business, or they can even limit your ability to take on loans or encumber the business in the future. Obviously, if there's no third-party investor, the decision for the company and management fall on you, the business owner, the buyer. You hold all the decision-making. But if you bring on a third-party investor, they may have a different perspective, a different vision, a different expectation for the business that you're buying. There are things you need to consider when you're thinking about bringing a third-party investor into your business. Now, if you want to go this route, that's perfectly fine. This is a great route to go if you get the right partner. But you must educate yourself on how to structure these deals so that you're free to operate in your business once you buy it. In the resources section of this lesson, we'll have included a PDF outlining several different scenarios for finding equity financing. However, if you're not sold on equity financing, keep watching. Coming up, I'll be covering even more ways to help finance your deals. I'll see you in the next lesson.
